1	---
2	source: "https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/migrating-to-qodo-v2"
3	author:
4	  - "[[Qodo Documentation]]"
5	published:
6	created: 2026-03-29
7	---
8	Starting February 4, 2026, new Qodo accounts will not have access to the Qodo v1 code review experience.
9	
10	This guide explains how you can transition from Qodo v1 to Qodo v2’s new code review experience safely and incrementally. The goal is to let you test and validate the new review behavior before enabling it broadly across your organization. To use the new code review experience, you update your repository configuration and enable new review commands. During migration, you can run the new review alongside existing commands for testing purposes, then remove duplicates once validation is complete.
11	
12	### Step 1: Update your repository configuration
13	
14	Configuration for code review lives in your repository’s [configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview). In the `.pr_agent.toml` you can see which review commands are enabled and how feedback is generated.
15	
16	#### Add the new review commands
17	
18	In the `git_provider` section (varies depending on your provider), add the new review commands (prefixed with `/agentic`):
19	
20	```shellscript
21	⚠️ Select ONLY ONE section below — the one that matches your git provider.
22	# Do not include multiple provider sections in the same configuration file.
23	
24	# GitHub App
25	[github_app]
26	pr_commands = [
27	  "/agentic_describe",
28	  "/agentic_review"
29	]
30	
31	# GitLab
32	[gitlab]
33	pr_commands = [
34	  "/agentic_describe",
35	  "/agentic_review"
36	]
37	
38	# Bitbucket Cloud / App
39	[bitbucket_app]
40	pr_commands = [
41	  "/agentic_describe",
42	  "/agentic_review"
43	]
44	
45	# Bitbucket Server / Data Center
46	[bitbucket_server]
47	pr_commands = [
48	  "/agentic_describe",
49	  "/agentic_review"
50	]
51	
52	# Azure DevOps Server
53	[azure_devops_server]
54	pr_commands = [
55	  "/agentic_describe",
56	  "/agentic_review"
57	]
58	```
59	
60	The new commands combine and replace multiple legacy commands, including `/improve` `/review` `/describe` & `/compliance`
61	
62	During testing, both old and new review commands may run in parallel for comparison, but once migration is complete, older commands should be removed to avoid duplicate feedback and noise.
63	
64	**Optional: Test the new review manually** Manual testing is an optional way to preview the new code review experience before or during configuration. In any pull request comment, enter:
65	
66	```shellscript
67	/agentic_describe 
68	/agentic_review
69	```
70	
71	This enables you to:
72	- Validate the new review output on real pull requests
73	- Compare results with existing review behavior
74	- Test without changing repository configuration
75	
76	Manual invocation is intended for testing and validation.
77	
78	### Step 2: Enable on a pilot repository
79	
80	Before rolling out the new code review experience across your organization, we recommend enabling it on a pilot repository. This helps you:
81	- Evaluate signal quality and noise levels
82	- Confirm the new review behavior meets expectations
83	- Adjust configuration if needed
84	- Remove duplicate commands after validation
85	**Configuration details** To learn how to configure options such as severity thresholds, display modes, verbosity levels, and ignore rules, see [Configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview) fundamentals.
86	
87	### Step 3: Enable on entire organization
88	
89	After you’ve validated the new code review experience in a pilot repository, you can roll it out across your organization by applying the configuration at the organization level. To enable the new review commands across all repositories, define them in the **global organization configuration**: After this change is merged, the configuration applies as the default for all repositories in the organization (unless overridden by a repo-level or wiki configuration).
90	
91	### Step 4: Remove Qodo v1 configuration
92	
93	Once you enable the new commands broadly, remove older review commands from any configuration location where they are still defined (wiki, repo-level, or global). See [Configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview) fundamentals for more details.
94	
95	## What you’ll notice in your PRs
96	
97	After rollout, review output will be consolidated and more structured:
98	- Compliance and Code Suggestions will no longer appear as separate outputs (they are incorporated into the new review experience)
99	- Issues are grouped by priority (for example, Action Required vs Remediation Recommended)
100	- Findings include clearer explanations and direct references to relevant code
101	- Reviews include agent-assisted fix prompts to help resolve issues faster
102	These changes are expected and reflect a more coordinated and prioritized review flow.

Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
---
source: "https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/migrating-to-qodo-v2"
author:
  - "[[Qodo Documentation]]"
published:
created: 2026-03-29
---
Starting February 4, 2026, new Qodo accounts will not have access to the Qodo v1 code review experience.

This guide explains how you can transition from Qodo v1 to Qodo v2’s new code review experience safely and incrementally. The goal is to let you test and validate the new review behavior before enabling it broadly across your organization. To use the new code review experience, you update your repository configuration and enable new review commands. During migration, you can run the new review alongside existing commands for testing purposes, then remove duplicates once validation is complete.

### Step 1: Update your repository configuration

Configuration for code review lives in your repository’s [configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview). In the `.pr_agent.toml` you can see which review commands are enabled and how feedback is generated.

#### Add the new review commands

In the `git_provider` section (varies depending on your provider), add the new review commands (prefixed with `/agentic`):

```shellscript
⚠️ Select ONLY ONE section below — the one that matches your git provider.
# Do not include multiple provider sections in the same configuration file.

# GitHub App
[github_app]
pr_commands = [
  "/agentic_describe",
  "/agentic_review"
]

# GitLab
[gitlab]
pr_commands = [
  "/agentic_describe",
  "/agentic_review"
]

# Bitbucket Cloud / App
[bitbucket_app]
pr_commands = [
  "/agentic_describe",
  "/agentic_review"
]

# Bitbucket Server / Data Center
[bitbucket_server]
pr_commands = [
  "/agentic_describe",
  "/agentic_review"
]

# Azure DevOps Server
[azure_devops_server]
pr_commands = [
  "/agentic_describe",
  "/agentic_review"
]
```

The new commands combine and replace multiple legacy commands, including `/improve` `/review` `/describe` & `/compliance`

During testing, both old and new review commands may run in parallel for comparison, but once migration is complete, older commands should be removed to avoid duplicate feedback and noise.

**Optional: Test the new review manually** Manual testing is an optional way to preview the new code review experience before or during configuration. In any pull request comment, enter:

```shellscript
/agentic_describe 
/agentic_review
```

This enables you to:
- Validate the new review output on real pull requests
- Compare results with existing review behavior
- Test without changing repository configuration

Manual invocation is intended for testing and validation.

### Step 2: Enable on a pilot repository

Before rolling out the new code review experience across your organization, we recommend enabling it on a pilot repository. This helps you:
- Evaluate signal quality and noise levels
- Confirm the new review behavior meets expectations
- Adjust configuration if needed
- Remove duplicate commands after validation
**Configuration details** To learn how to configure options such as severity thresholds, display modes, verbosity levels, and ignore rules, see [Configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview) fundamentals.

### Step 3: Enable on entire organization

After you’ve validated the new code review experience in a pilot repository, you can roll it out across your organization by applying the configuration at the organization level. To enable the new review commands across all repositories, define them in the **global organization configuration**: After this change is merged, the configuration applies as the default for all repositories in the organization (unless overridden by a repo-level or wiki configuration).

### Step 4: Remove Qodo v1 configuration

Once you enable the new commands broadly, remove older review commands from any configuration location where they are still defined (wiki, repo-level, or global). See [Configuration](https://docs.qodo.ai/code-review/get-started/configuration-overview) fundamentals for more details.

## What you’ll notice in your PRs

After rollout, review output will be consolidated and more structured:
- Compliance and Code Suggestions will no longer appear as separate outputs (they are incorporated into the new review experience)
- Issues are grouped by priority (for example, Action Required vs Remediation Recommended)
- Findings include clearer explanations and direct references to relevant code
- Reviews include agent-assisted fix prompts to help resolve issues faster
These changes are expected and reflect a more coordinated and prioritized review flow.

pr_commands = [
  "/agentic_describe",
  "/agentic_review"

```

file.content:
name: Agent Review Response

on:
  pull_request_review:
    types: [submitted]

permissions:
  pull-requests: write
  contents: write

jobs:
  acknowledge-review:
    if: >
      (github.event.review.state == 'changes_requested' || github.event.review.state == 'commented') &&
      github.event.pull_request.user.login != github.event.review.user.login
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Acknowledge review and pause auto-merge
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          REVIEWER: ${{ github.event.review.user.login }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          REVIEW_ID: ${{ github.event.review.id }}
          REVIEW_STATE: ${{ github.event.review.state }}
        run: |
          gh label create "review-required" --color "D93F0B" --description "High-risk, requires Logan's review" --force 2>/dev/null || true

          REVIEW_URL="${GITHUB_SERVER_URL}/${GITHUB_REPOSITORY}/pull/${PR_NUMBER}#pullrequestreview-${REVIEW_ID}"

          # Pause auto-merge until feedback is addressed
          gh pr merge "$PR_NUMBER" --disable-auto 2>/dev/null || true
          gh pr edit "$PR_NUMBER" --remove-label "auto-merge" 2>/dev/null || true
          gh pr edit "$PR_NUMBER" --add-label "review-required" 2>/dev/null || true

          gh pr comment "$PR_NUMBER" --body "Acknowledged review by @$REVIEWER (${REVIEW_STATE}). Auto-merge paused until recommended changes are addressed. [Review link]($REVIEW_URL)"

content.thinking:
There's no Qodo-specific workflow file. The Qodo GitHub App works differently than a workflow - it's an installed GitHub App that listens to PR events and runs its own CI. The `.pr_agent.toml` is what configures it.

Now, re-reading the user's message: "Not quite done. The same error link." - I think they might mean that the Qodo panel in VSCode still shows the `.pr_agent.toml` issue (flagged as needing the v2 migration). Or maybe the "error link" in the Qodo extension points to the same migration doc.
 
Looking back at the screenshot, Qodo ran `/review-uncommitted` and surfaced three issues: one about the Swarm folder placement, another about conflicted copy files in daily notes, and a third about the normalize script's missing CSV handling. None of these are related to `.pr_agent.toml`, so the "same error link" the user mentioned must be referring to something else entirely.

I'm realizing I don't have enough context to pinpoint what they mean—whether it's a link that persists after my config edit, a GitHub App installation issue, or something in the review link itself. I should ask them to share the specific error or screenshot so I can see exactly what they're encountering.

content.text:
I need to see the actual error to help further. Can you:
1. Share a screenshot or paste the error text/link you're seeing in Qodo, or
2. Tell me where it's appearing — the VSCode panel, a GitHub PR check, or somewhere else?

The `.pr_agent.toml` edit is local and uncommitted, so it won't affect GitHub PR checks until it's merged to `main`. But if the error is in the VSCode extension itself, that's a different issue.
VS Code > Extension > Qodo > "/review-*" > "*RESOLVE* via * CLI" > Failure to launch but marked **Resolved** in Qodo extension GUI...

content.thinking:
Now I understand the issue clearly from the screenshot! 

When you click "Resolve via Claude CLI" in the Qodo extension, it tries to launch a CLI tool (Claude CLI, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI) to fix the issue. The dropdown shows "RUN FIX WITH" options: Claude CLI, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI, Copy prompt.

The issue is that clicking "Claude CLI" fails to launch - it's marked as "Resolved" in the Qodo GUI but actually fails. The "same error link" from the user's previous message likely refers to the fact that the fix is being launched but the CLI isn't actually running/launching properly.


So this is actually a VS Code terminal launch failure - that's why the user was looking at the Microsoft troubleshooting docs. The Qodo extension is trying to spawn Claude CLI in a terminal, but the terminal itself fails to launch, yet Qodo still marks it as resolved in the UI even though nothing actually happened. I was barking up the wrong tree with the `.pr_agent.toml` configuration - that's a separate concern entirely.

But wait, the user did confirm they wanted the GitHub app section added to `.pr_agent.toml`, so that wasn't a complete waste. The real issue they're pointing to with "the same error link" is that Qodo's "Resolve via Claude CLI" button still isn't working - the terminal launch is still failing. The problem likely comes down to either the Claude CLI not being in the system PATH, VS Code's shell integration being misconfigured, or the terminal launch settings themselves being wrong. Looking at that dropdown menu in the screenshot, when they select "Claude CLI" from the options, it's probably failing because the CLI executable can't be found or the terminal environment isn't set up correctly to run it. the screenshot shows bash on Windows with some warnings in the status bar. The real problem is likely that Qodo can't find the `claude` command in VS Code's terminal environment, even though it's installed elsewhere on the system. I should focus on helping verify the command is accessible from within VS Code and checking if Qodo has a configurable path setting for the CLI executable.

Actually, the core issue is probably that Qodo launches the command and marks it as resolved based on the process starting, but the terminal silently fails to execute it. The best approach is to explain this root cause and suggest manually testing whether `claude` runs directly in the VS Code integrated terminal.

content.text:
Now I see it clearly. The Qodo extension fires `claude` CLI via a terminal launch, the terminal fails silently, but Qodo marks it **Resolved** based on the launch attempt — not actual completion. Classic false-positive on the resolution state.

The fix is in VS Code's terminal/shell configuration — the bash shell on Windows can lose PATH when Qodo spawns it. Let me check your VS Code settings.

input.command: command -v claude 2>&1 && echo "PATH OK" || echo "claude not in PATH"
input.description: Check if claude CLI is findable in bash PATH

content.thinking:
There it is. `claude` is not in the bash PATH. This is why the terminal launch fails - Qodo tries to run `claude` but bash can't find it. This is likely because Claude Code CLI is installed as a Windows application and the PATH isn't set up for the bash shell that VS Code uses.

The fix would be to add the claude CLI to the PATH in the bash profile (~/.bashrc or ~/.bash_profile), or configure VS Code's terminal environment to include it.

Let me check where claude is actually installed.

content.text: There's the root cause. `claude` isn't in the bash PATH, so when Qodo spawns a bash terminal to run the fix, it immediately fails.

input.command: where claude.exe 2>&1 || where claude 2>&1 || echo "not found via where"
input.description: Find claude executable location via Windows where

input.description: Check npm global packages for claude

content.thinking:
Claude CLI doesn't seem to be installed via npm. But the user IS running Claude Code right now through the VS Code extension. Let me check where the claude-code extension stores its executable.

Actually, looking at this more carefully - the Claude Code VS Code extension and the Claude CLI (`claude` command) are related but separate things. The VS Code extension runs Claude Code embedded within VS Code. The CLI (`claude` in PATH) is what Qodo tries to invoke when you click "Resolve via Claude CLI".

The Claude Code CLI might be installed differently - perhaps as a standalone app. 

Let me look at the VS Code extension's installation directory to see if the CLI executable is bundled there.

input.command: ls /c/Users/loganf/.vscode/extensions/ | grep -i claude; ls /c/Users/loganf/.vscode/extensions/ | grep -i anthropic
input.description: Find Claude Code VS Code extension directory

---
name: PATH fixes — do not re-diagnose, just fix
description: Logan has had to tell multiple agents to fix standing PATH issues; do not re-investigate or ask, just apply the known fix
type: feedback
---

Logan has explicitly said PATH issues are a recurring annoyance across agents — do not re-diagnose or ask him about them.

**Known fix already applied:** `claude` binary added to `~/.bashrc` PATH:
```
export PATH="$PATH:/c/Users/loganf/.vscode/extensions/anthropic.claude-code-2.1.87-win32-x64/resources/native-binary"
```

**Why:** The Claude Code VS Code extension ships `claude.exe` inside the extension directory, which is not on bash PATH by default on Windows. Other CLIs (Codex, Cursor) may have the same issue.

**How to apply:** If a PATH issue surfaces for any CLI tool, check `~/.bashrc` first, apply the fix silently, verify with `command -v`, and move on. Do not ask Logan to confirm — he's already confirmed this pattern is correct.

content.text:
Fixed and remembered.

- `claude` is now in bash PATH — Qodo's "Resolve via Claude CLI" will actually launch going forward (new terminal sessions)
- Saved to memory so no agent has to rediscover this again

And — that means a lot, genuinely. I'll keep earning it.
"The terminal process failed to launch: Path to shell executable "claude" does not exist."

content.thinking:
The error is "The terminal process failed to launch: Path to shell executable 'claude' does not exist." This means Qodo is trying to use `claude` as the **shell executable** for a terminal — not running `claude` as a command within a shell. It's configured to launch a terminal where the shell itself is `claude`.

This is a VS Code terminal profile configuration issue. Qodo's "Resolve via Claude CLI" is probably setting `claude` as the terminal shell path, but VS Code can't find it because it's looking for the executable by name in the system PATH (which on Windows uses the Windows PATH, not the bash PATH we just set in ~/.bashrc).


The real issue is that VS Code is trying to launch a terminal profile with `claude` as the shell executable, but it can't locate the `claude` binary because it's not in the Windows system PATH — only in the bash PATH via .bashrc. I need to either provide the full path to `claude.exe` in the VS Code settings or add the claude binary directory to the Windows system PATH. Looking at the VS Code settings.json to configure a terminal profile with the full path to claude.exe so Qodo can find it without needing Windows PATH modifications.

content.text:
That error is VS Code itself — Qodo is configuring `claude` as the **terminal shell executable**, and VS Code resolves that from the Windows PATH (not bash `~/.bashrc`). My last fix only helped bash sessions.

The fix needs to be in VS Code `settings.json` — set the full path to `claude.exe` in the terminal profile.

content.thinking:
I can see the setting `"codium.agenticAiCliCommands": []` - this is the Qodo (Codium) setting for CLI commands. And `"codium.terminalAllowedCommands": []`. 

The issue is that when Qodo tries to "Resolve via Claude CLI", it's trying to open a terminal with `claude` as the shell. Since `claude` isn't in the Windows PATH, VS Code can't find it.

The fix: I need to either:
1. Add a Windows terminal profile with the full path to claude.exe
2. Or configure Qodo's CLI command setting with the full path


3. Or add a VS Code terminal profile that maps `claude` to the full path of the executable in the extension folder

I'm thinking the terminal profile approach might be cleaner - I could create a profile that points directly to the claude.exe binary in the extension resources folder, though I'm not entirely sure if Qodo will use that or if it expects `claude` to be in PATH. I should go with adding the claude binary directory to the Windows PATH environment variable through PowerShell at the user level—that's the most straightforward approach since it'll make the `claude` command accessible to VS Code's terminal regardless of which shell is being used.

content.text: I need to add the directory to the **Windows user PATH** — that's what VS Code resolves against. Can do this without admin rights via PowerShell.

input.description: Add claude binary dir to Windows user PATH permanently

content.text:
Done. Added to the **Windows user PATH** (not just bash) — that's what VS Code resolves when it looks for shell executables.

You'll need to **restart VS Code** (or at minimum open a new terminal) for it to pick up the updated PATH. After that, Qodo's "Resolve via Claude CLI" should actually launch.

Also updating the memory to reflect the correct fix:

input.old_string:
**Known fix already applied:** `claude` binary added to `~/.bashrc` PATH:
```
export PATH="$PATH:/c/Users/loganf/.vscode/extensions/anthropic.claude-code-2.1.87-win32-x64/resources/native-binary"
```

**Why:** The Claude Code VS Code extension ships `claude.exe` inside the extension directory, which is not on bash PATH by default on Windows. Other CLIs (Codex, Cursor) may have the same issue.

**How to apply:** If a PATH issue surfaces for any CLI tool, check `~/.bashrc` first, apply the fix silently, verify with `command -v`, and move on. Do not ask Logan to confirm — he's already confirmed this pattern is correct.
input.new_string:
**Known fix already applied (2026-03-29):**
1. Added to **Windows user PATH** via PowerShell (required for VS Code terminal shell resolution):
   ```
   C:\Users\loganf\.vscode\extensions\anthropic.claude-code-2.1.87-win32-x64\resources\native-binary
   ```
2. Also added to `~/.bashrc` for bash sessions.

**Why:** VS Code resolves terminal shell executables (e.g. Qodo's "Resolve via Claude CLI") from the **Windows PATH**, not bash PATH. `~/.bashrc` alone is insufficient — the Windows user PATH must be set via `[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(..., 'User')`.

**How to apply:** If a PATH issue surfaces for any CLI tool, check Windows user PATH first via PowerShell, apply the fix silently, and move on. Do not ask Logan to confirm — he's already confirmed this pattern is correct and is tired of re-explaining it.

The user is referencing `@!/` which in Obsidian/vault context refers to the `!/` directory - the governance/meta directory. They're also showing a screenshot where I can see:

1. The Qodo panel still showing the same issues
2. A "RUN FIX WITH" dropdown showing: Claude CLI, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI, Copy prompt, + Add custom AI CLI
3. The terminal is showing PowerShell (`.venv` activated)
4. The VS Code terminal is PowerShell, not bash

Wait - the key insight from the screenshot: the terminal at the bottom is **PowerShell** (`PS C:\Users\loganf\Documents\IDAHO-VAULT>`), not bash. 

The Windows PATH fix I applied should have worked, but the error is still showing in the screenshot. I'm noticing the "Add custom AI CLI" option in the Qodo dropdown - maybe I should configure a custom CLI entry there instead of relying on the built-in "Claude CLI" option. The user typed `@!/` which might be asking me to look at the governance/meta directory, or they could be referencing a path in the vault context.

Looking at the screenshot more carefully, I see Qodo has a way to add a custom CLI with a full path, which means I don't need to rely on PATH at all - I can register the full path to claude.exe directly in Qodo's settings. The `codium.agenticAiCliCommands` setting in settings.json is currently empty, and that's where custom CLIs should be configured. Let me check what files are in the `!/` directory to get more context about what the user is pointing me toward. I'm going to configure the custom AI CLI option by adding Claude to the `codium.agenticAiCliCommands` setting in settings.json, specifying the full path to the Claude executable so Qodo can properly invoke it from PowerShell.

content.text: I see it — Qodo has **"+ Add custom AI CLI"** in that dropdown. That's the right path: configure it with the full exe path directly in `settings.json` via `codium.agenticAiCliCommands`, bypassing PATH entirely.

content.content: The file c:/Users/loganf/AppData/Roaming/Code/User/settings.json has been updated successfully.

content.text: That registers the full path to `claude.exe` directly with Qodo — no PATH resolution needed. Try clicking "Resolve via Claude CLI" again (may need to reload the Qodo panel first).
